556	       minimal-resolution carving
557	   (Stacks lemma-add-trivial-complex)
558	                  |
559	                  v
560	                gap (3)
561	        snake lemma on minimal
562	        resolution under x in m
563	                  |
564	                  v
565	   +--------------+--------------+
566	   |   L1299 closure assembly    |   <--   gap (2)
567	   |   (succ-pd inductive step)  |        Stacks 00MF
568	   +-----------------------------+        "what is exact"

581	\paragraph{Per-gap effort estimates.} The following table records,
582	for each gap, the estimated formalisation effort (lines of code in
583	the project's Lean source style), a coarse iter budget, the
584	Mathlib-at-\texttt{b80f227} status, and substrate dependencies on

604	The gap (1) estimate is anchored to Bruns--Herzog,
605	\emph{Cohen--Macaulay Rings}, Construction~1.5.18 together with the
606	minimal-resolution induction (\S1.5): the trimming algorithm is a
607	finite-rank linear-algebra computation followed by induction on the
608	length of the resolution. The gap (2) estimate reflects the chain
609	Bruns--Herzog Theorem~1.4.13 \(\to\) Eagon--Northcott
610	\(\to\) Buchsbaum--Eisenbud's exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
611	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}), which characterises exactness of a
612	complex of finite free modules in terms of depths of the ideals of
613	\(r\)-minors of the transition matrices. Gap (2) is the largest of
614	the three open substrates and is a natural candidate for an upstream

617	\paragraph{Gap (3) OBVIATED iter-200.} The iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB
618	pivot (\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the literal
619	\(\texttt{ChainComplex}\,\mathbb N\) carrier with the abstract
620	\(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES-descent pattern; under this
621	pattern, minimality of the resolution is \emph{not} used as input
622	anywhere in the inductive step. Consequently the snake-lemma carving
623	of the minimal resolution (gap (3)) is no longer a substrate
624	requirement: only gap (2) (\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow
625	\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)) remains the binding gap for
626	the closure of \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}, and the
627	per-syzygy step of gap (1) is consumed only through the SES at
628	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) (no minimality input required).
629	See \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot} for the SES-descent details.
630	
631	\paragraph{Gap (2) Stacks 00MF proof recipe.} The
632	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
633	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}) reads: a complex
634	\(\dots \to R^{r_2} \xrightarrow{\varphi_2} R^{r_1}
635	 \xrightarrow{\varphi_1} R^{r_0}\) of finite free modules over a
636	Noetherian local ring \(R\) is exact at \(R^{r_i}\) (for \(i \geq 1\))
637	if and only if \(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi_{i+1} + \texttt{rank}\,\varphi_i
638	= r_i\) and \(\texttt{depth}\,I(\varphi_i) \geq i\), where
639	\(I(\varphi)\) denotes the ideal of \(r\)-minors of \(\varphi\) for
640	\(r = \texttt{rank}\,\varphi\). The recipe for an iter-201+ prover:
641	(a) define \(I(\varphi) \subseteq R\) as the ideal of all
642	\(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi\)-minors of \(\varphi\) (Mathlib supports
643	this via \(\texttt{Matrix.minors}\) and \(\texttt{Ideal.span}\) over the
644	finite index set of \(\binom{n}{r}\) tuples); (b) prove the depth
645	lower bound via induction on \(i\) using a Koszul-complex argument
646	on the regular sequence implicit in the exactness data; (c) derive
647	\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow \texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)
648	as a corollary by taking the start \(\dots \to R^{r_1} \to R^{r_0} \to
649	M\) of a minimal resolution and applying the criterion at \(i = 1\).
650	Estimated \(\sim 150-200\) LOC project-side; Mathlib upstream PR
651	candidate.
652	
653	\paragraph{Closure assembly (iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot).} The
654	iter-200 substrate landing in \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}
655	replaces the original (1)+(3)-dependent assembly with an SES-descent
656	recipe: given an \(M\) with \(\texttt{pd}_R(M) = k+1\), the
657	per-syzygy step of gap (1)
658	(\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}) supplies a
659	surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\); the SES
660	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) plus the iter-200 helpers
661	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker\_of\_surjection}\) +
662	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_succ\_of\_hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker}\)
663	give \(\texttt{pd}_R(\ker f) = k\) exactly; gap (2) (the
664	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud criterion at the start of a minimal resolution)
665	delivers \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\); the iter-200
666	helper
667	\(\texttt{depth\_ker\_ge\_min\_of\_surjection\_finite\_localRing}\)
668	gives \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f \geq \min(\texttt{depth}\,R,
669	\texttt{depth}\,M + 1) = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\); a complementary LES
670	upper bound (or the IH applied to \(\ker f\) under the strict
671	descent of \(\texttt{pd}\)) gives the matching upper bound. Combine
672	to get \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\), then apply
673	the IH (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\)) for
674	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), which rearranges
675	to \((k+1) + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) as required.
676	Estimated closure-assembly cost (gaps in hand): \(\sim 50-80\) LOC
677	inductive code.
678	
679	\paragraph{Iter budget refinement.} Aggregating the per-gap
680	estimates with the closure assembly: gap (1) at 1--2 iters, gap (2)
681	at 2--3 iters, gap (3) at 1--2 iters, and the closure assembly
682	at 1 iter gives a total remaining iter budget of \textbf{5--8 iters}

698	\subsection{Gap (1) first-step substrate (iter-199): per-syzygy
699	  minimal surjection from a finite free module}
700	\label{subsec:ab_gap1_first_step}
701	
702	\begin{lemma}
703	
704	\leanok
705	  [Per-syzygy minimal surjection from \(R^n\) for finite modules
706	    over a local ring]
707	  \label{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
708	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
709	  \uses{def:depth}
710	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
711	  % (lemma-add-trivial-complex; minimal-resolution carving substrate)
712	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L24590-L24623).
713	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
714	  % "Suppose $R$ is a ring. Let
715	  %     \ldots
716	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 1}}
717	  %     R^{n_i}
718	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_i}
719	  %     R^{n_{i-1}}
720	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i-1}}
721	  %     \ldots
722	  %  be a complex of finite free $R$-modules. Suppose that for some $i$
723	  %  some matrix coefficient of the map $\varphi_i$ is invertible.
724	  %  Then the displayed complex is isomorphic to the direct sum of a
725	  %  complex
726	  %     \ldots \to
727	  %     R^{n_{i + 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 2}}
728	  %     R^{n_{i + 1}} \to
729	  %     R^{n_i - 1} \to
730	  %     R^{n_{i - 1} - 1} \to
731	  %     R^{n_{i - 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i - 2}}
732	  %     R^{n_{i - 3}} \to
733	  %     \ldots
734	  %  and the complex $\ldots \to 0 \to R \to R \to 0 \to \ldots$ where
735	  %  the map $R \to R$ is the identity map."
736	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
737	    (\texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}).}
738	  Let \(R\) be a local ring (Noetherian-ness NOT required at this
739	  step), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) the maximal ideal,
740	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\) the residue field, and \(M\) a finite
741	  \(R\)-module. Then there exist \(n \in \mathbb N\) and an
742	  \(R\)-linear surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) such that:
743	  \begin{enumerate}
744	    \item \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\) (the minimal
745	      generator count of \(M\), via Nakayama);
746	    \item \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) (the
747	      ``minimality'' condition: every entry of every relation lives
748	      in the maximal ideal, so the chosen lift has no trivial
749	      summands).
750	  \end{enumerate}
751	\end{lemma}
752	\begin{proof}
753	
754	\leanok
755	  Pick a \(\kappa\)-basis
756	  \(b : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \kappa \otimes_R M\) via
757	  \texttt{Module.finBasis} with
758	  \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\). Surjectivity of the
759	  canonical map \(M \twoheadrightarrow \kappa \otimes_R M\)
760	  (composition of \texttt{TensorProduct.mk\_surjective} and
761	  \texttt{Ideal.Quotient.mk\_surjective}) gives lifts
762	  \(m_i \in M\) with \(1 \otimes m_i = b_i\). Define
763	  \(f : R^n \to M\) by \(f x := \sum_i x_i \cdot m_i\) via
764	  \texttt{(Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m}.
765	
766	  Surjectivity: \(\mathrm{range}\, f = \mathrm{span}_R(m_i)\) by
767	  \texttt{Module.Basis.constr\_range}, and
768	  \(\mathrm{span}_R(m_i) = M\) by Nakayama via
769	  \texttt{IsLocalRing.span\_eq\_top\_of\_tmul\_eq\_basis}.
770	
771	  Kernel containment: for \(x \in \ker f\) the relation
772	  \(\sum_i x_i m_i = 0\) tensors to
773	  \(\sum_i (1 \otimes x_i \cdot m_i) = 0\); using
774	  \texttt{TensorProduct.tmul\_smul} and the linear independence of
775	  \(b\) (\texttt{Module.Basis.linearIndependent}) we obtain
776	  \(\mathrm{residue}_R(x_i) = 0\) for every \(i\), i.e.\
777	  \(x_i \in \mathfrak m\). Decomposing
778	  \(x = \sum_i x_i \cdot \mathrm{Pi.single}\,i\,1\) then yields
779	  \(x \in \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) by
780	  \texttt{Submodule.smul\_mem\_smul} and
781	  \texttt{Submodule.sum\_mem}.
782	\end{proof}
783	
784	This lemma is the \emph{per-syzygy} step of gap (1)'s
785	minimal-resolution carving: given a finite \(R\)-module \(M\)
786	over a local ring \(R\), it produces a minimal surjection
787	\(R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\). Iter-200's ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot
788	(\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the original
789	\(\mathbb N\)-recursive \(\texttt{ChainComplex}\) assembly target

910	split \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} into an

912	\emph{base case} (\(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\)). Each piece uses only
913	already-existing LES infrastructure plus one new

915	
916	\textit{Inductive step \(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)
917	\((\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC).} From
918	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker_of_surjection} +
919	the contrapositive of
920	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_succ_of_hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker},
921	\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\) exactly. Strong induction gives
922	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), hence
923	\(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f < \texttt{depth}\,R\) since \(k \geq 1\).
924	Lower bound \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\):
925	\(\texttt{depth\_of\_short\_exact}\) part (2). Upper bound
926	\(\texttt{depth}\,M \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\): by
927	contradiction --- assume \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\),
928	look at the LES of \(\texttt{Ext}^*(\kappa, -)\) at
929	\(i = \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\), infer
930	\(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,\ker f}(\kappa, R^n) \neq 0\)
931	(forcing \(\texttt{depth}\,R \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\)),
932	contradicting the IH.

934	\textit{Base case \(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\) \((\sim 80\)--\(120\) LOC).}
935	\(\ker f\) is projective (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = 0\)), hence free
936	over the local Noetherian \(R\) by Mathlib's
937	\(\texttt{Module.free\_of\_flat\_of\_isLocalRing}\) +
938	\(\texttt{Module.Flat.of\_projective}\). So \(\ker f \cong R^m\) with
939	\(m \geq 1\). The inclusion \(\ker f \hookrightarrow R^n\) is an
940	\((n \times m)\)-matrix \(A\) with entries in
941	\(\texttt{Ann}(\kappa) = \mathfrak m\) (from
942	\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}'s minimality
943	clause \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\)).

945	The \textbf{matrix-collapse helper}
946	(CLOSED iter-201, 4 axiom-clean private decls at
947	\texttt{AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} L1418--L1517):
948	\[
949	  \forall p \geq 0,\quad \forall e \in \texttt{Ext}^p(\kappa, R^n),\quad
950	  e \circ \texttt{Ext.mk}_0\,(\texttt{ofHom}\,A) = 0
951	  \quad \text{in } \texttt{Ext}^{p+0}(\kappa, R^n)
952	  \text{ when } A_{ij} \in \texttt{Ann}_R\,\kappa.
953	\]
954	The 4 helpers are \(\texttt{Module.elemMap}\) (the elementary map
955	sending \(\texttt{Pi.single}\,j\,1 \mapsto \texttt{Pi.single}\,i\,1\)
956	and other basis vectors to zero), \(\texttt{Module.elemMap\_apply}\),
957	\(\texttt{Module.linearMap\_finFunR\_matrix\_decomp}\)
958	(\(A = \sum_{i,j} A(\texttt{Pi.single}\,j\,1)\,i \cdot
959	   \texttt{elemMap}_{ij}\)), and the closure helper
960	\(\texttt{Module.ext\_comp\_mk}_0\texttt{\_ofHom\_eq\_zero\_of\_entries\_mem\_annihilator}\)
961	itself. The realised API path uses Mathlib's
962	\(\texttt{ModuleCat.hom\_sum}\) + \(\texttt{Ext.mk}_0\texttt{\_sum}\)
963	+ \(\texttt{Ext.comp\_sum}\) and the scalar siblings
964	\(\texttt{ModuleCat.hom\_smul}\) + \(\texttt{Ext.mk}_0\texttt{\_smul}\)
965	+ \(\texttt{Ext.comp\_smul}\) (\emph{not}
966	\(\texttt{Ext.bilinearCompOfLinear}\) as originally projected; the
967	mathematical content is identical). Each summand reduces to a scalar-
968	bilinear form killed by the existing
969	\(\texttt{ext\_smul\_eq\_zero\_of\_mem\_annihilator}\) (L229).
970	
971	With matrix-collapse, the LES at \(i = \texttt{depth}\,R - 1\)
972	gives
973	\[
974	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R - 1}(\kappa, M)
975	  \xrightarrow{\;\delta\;}
976	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f)
977	  \xrightarrow{\;(- \circ A)\;}
978	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, R^n)
979	\]
980	with the second arrow zero. If
981	\(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,R\), the source is zero,
982	hence \(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f) = 0\) ---
983	but \(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f) \simeq
984	\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, R^m) \neq 0\)
985	(\(m \geq 1\), \(\texttt{depth}\,R^m = \texttt{depth}\,R\)).
986	Contradiction. So \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\), and the
987	base case
988	\(\texttt{pd}\,M + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) follows
989	from \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\) (since \(\ker f\)
990	free).

1032	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
1033	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
1034	
1035	\begin{definition}
1036	
1037	\leanok
1038	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
1039	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
1040	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
1041	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
1042	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
1043	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1044	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
1045	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
1046	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
1047	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
1048	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
1049	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
1050	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
1051	\end{definition}
1052	
1053	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
1054	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
1055	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
1056	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
1057	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
1058	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
1059	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
1060	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
1061	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
1062	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
1063	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
1064	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
1065	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
1066	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add
1067	% that hint). The Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap remains the load-bearing substrate;
1068	% iter-186+ work paths unchanged: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in project
1069	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;
1070	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
1071	\begin{corollary}
1072	
1073	\leanok
1074	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
1075	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
1076	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
1077	  
1078	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
1079	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
1080	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1081	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
1082	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
1083	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
1084	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
1085	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
1086	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
1087	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
1088	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
1089	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
1090	\end{corollary}
1091	
1092	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1093	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
1094	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
1095	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
1096	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
1097	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
1098	\begin{proof}
1099	  
1100	  
1101	  
1102	  
1103	  
1104	\leanok
1105	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
1106	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
1107	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
1108	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
1109	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
1110	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
1111	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
1112	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
1113	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
1114	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
1115	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
1116	
1117	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
1118	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
1119	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
1120	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
1121	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
1122	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
1123	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
1124	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
1125	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
1126	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
1127	\end{proof}
1128	
1129	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
1130	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
1131	
1132	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
1133	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
1134	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
1135	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
1136	
1137	\begin{enumerate}
1138	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
1139	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
1140	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
1141	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
1142	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
1143	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
1144	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
1145	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
1146	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
1147	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
1148	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
1149	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
1150	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
1151	\end{enumerate}
1152	
1153	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
1154	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
1155	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
1156	Corollary~REF are formalized in
1157	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
1158	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
1159	
1160	\section{Lean encoding}
1161	\label{sec:ab_lean_encoding}

8	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
9	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
10	
11	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
12	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
13	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
14	\[
15	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
16	  \qquad
17	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
18	\]
19	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
20	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
21	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
22	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
23	
24	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
25	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
26	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
27	% groups)
28	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
29	% SOURCE QUOTE:
30	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
31	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
32	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
33	% abelian groups defined by the formula
34	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
35	% \end{dfn}
36	%  [\dots]
37	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
38	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
39	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
40	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
41	% topologies  by
42	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
43	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
44	%  \end{dfn}"
45	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
46	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
47	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
48	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
49	homomorphisms.}
50	
51	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
52	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
53	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
54	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
55	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
56	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
57	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
58	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
59	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
60	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
61	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
62	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
63	structure. This is the content of
64	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
65	
66	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
67	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
68	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
69	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
70	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
71	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
72	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
73	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
74	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
75	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
76	\begin{enumerate}
77	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
78	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
79	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
80	    isomorphism classes;
81	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
82	    for \(\otimes\);
83	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
84	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
85	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
86	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
87	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
88	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
89	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
90	\end{enumerate}
91	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
92	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
93	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
94	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
95	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
96	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
97	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
98	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
99	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
100	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
101	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
102	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
103	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
104	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
105	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
106	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
107	supplied.
108	
109	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
110	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
111	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
112	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
113	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
114	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
115	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
116	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
117	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
118	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
119	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
120	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
121	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
122	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
123	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
124	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
125	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
126	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
127	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
128	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
129	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
130	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
131	avoids. The
132	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
133	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
134	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
135	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
136	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
137	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
138	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
139	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
140	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
141	
142	\section{Mathlib API survey}
143	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
144	
145	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
146	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
147	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
148	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
149	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
150	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
151	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
152	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
153	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
154	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
155	source.
156	
157	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
158	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
159	monoidal-category instance
160	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
161	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
162	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
163	\[
164	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
165	\]

167	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
168	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
169	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).

182	\texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}, in

184	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
185	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,

187	\(\mathtt{Sheaf.val}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) carrier (equal by \(\mathtt{rfl}\)),
188	so this monoidal structure applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
189	\(\mathcal{O}_X\) --- no project work. This is the presheaf-level analogue of

191	
192	\paragraph{(ii) The localization-of-monoidal-categories API.} Mathlib's
193	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Basic} provides the class
194	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) and the construction
195	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\). The class records exactly
196	the whiskering-stability data:

198	% `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal`, in
199	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean` (L44, L86, L440)
200	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean).
201	% SOURCE QUOTE:
202	% "class IsMonoidal : Prop extends W.IsMultiplicative where
203	%   whiskerLeft (X : C) {Y₁ Y₂ : C} (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (hg : W g) : W (X ◁ g)
204	%   whiskerRight {X₁ X₂ : C} (f : X₁ ⟶ X₂) (hf : W f) (Y : C) : W (f ▷ Y)

242	\texttt{ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal\_W}, in

249	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
250	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
251	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
252	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
253	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
254	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on

256	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),

259	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
260	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
261	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
262	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
263	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
264	the \emph{full} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or a coherent monoidal category.
265	The \emph{full monoidal-category} route~(e) is therefore off the critical path. The
266	associator does, however, consume a \emph{single} left-whisker existence lemma
267	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
268	commutation; so the stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) itself is on the critical path, even
269	though the full coherent monoidal category built from it is not.
270	
271	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
272	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
273	is already present in Mathlib: the file
274	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
275	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
276	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
277	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
278	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
279	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
280	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
281	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
282	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
283	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
284	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
285	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. The full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent monoidal category that route~(e) would
286	build is not pursued; but the (d.1)-linearity packaging and the (d.2) stalk--tensor
287	commutation are \emph{on} the critical path, since the associator's single open
288	left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} is closed
289	precisely by (d.2) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}). What is superseded is only the
290	\emph{pair}-of-whiskering-fields \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) packaging and the
291	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, not the stalk infrastructure.
292	
293	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
294	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
295	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
296	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
297	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
298	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
299	independently of any upstream timing.
300	
301	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
302	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
303	
304	\begin{definition}
305	
306	
307	\leanok
308	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
309	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
311	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
312	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
313	  \[
314	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
315	  \]
316	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
317	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
318	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
319	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
320	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
321	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
322	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
323	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
324	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
325	  canonical compatibility
326	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
327	     \;\cong\;
328	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
329	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
330	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
331	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
332	
333	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
334	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
335	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
336	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
337	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
338	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
339	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
340	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{lemma}
344	
345	
346	\leanok
347	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
348	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
350	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
351	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
352	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
353	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
354	  \[
355	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
357	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
358	  \]
359	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
360	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
361	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
362	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
363	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
364	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
365	  \[
366	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
367	    \qquad
368	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
369	  \]
370	  the associator
371	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
372	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
373	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
374	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
375	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
376	\end{lemma}
377	
378	\begin{proof}
379	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
380	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
381	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
382	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
383	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
384	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
385	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
386	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
387	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
388	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
389	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
390	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
391	\end{proof}
392	
393	\begin{remark}
394	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
395	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
396	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
397	        lem:whisker_of_W}
398	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
399	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
400	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
7	% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
8	% codim-\(\geq 2\) extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),
9	% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary
10	% ``regular local \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay''
11	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\),
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
25	% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved
26	% here (see "Notes for Plan Agent" in the writer report).
27	%
28	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}
29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
81	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
82	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
83	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
84	
85	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
86	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
89	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
90	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
91	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
92	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
93	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
94	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
95	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
96	  \end{enumerate}
97	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
98	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
99	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
100	\end{proof}
101	
102	\begin{remark}
103	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
104	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
105	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
106	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-\(1\) step
107	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
108	  \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal
109	  and \(A\) is complete) and a codim-\(\geq 2\) step (depth-\(\geq 2\) at codim-\(\geq 2\) points,
110	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
111	  depth-\(\geq 2\) input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
112	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
113	  for a regular local ring \((\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)\) of Krull dimension \(d\), the
114	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to \(M = R\)
115	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
116	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d\), so in particular
117	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\) whenever \(\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2\).
118	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
119	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), every closed point \(x\) is regular of
120	  dimension \(2\), so \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(2\); sections of
121	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(U = X \setminus \{x\}\) extend uniquely across \(x\), and the same is
122	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of \(\mathcal O_A\) along \(f\).
123	
124	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
125	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-\(\geq 2\) half. The
126	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} dependency is recorded on
127	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
128	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
129	\end{remark}
130	
131	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
132	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
133	
134	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
135	\begin{itemize}
136	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
137	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
138	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
139	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
140	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
141	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
142	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
143	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
144	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
145	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
146	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
147	    has a characteristic restriction.
148	\end{itemize}
149	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
150	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
151	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
152	
153	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
154	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
155	
156	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
157	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\). Milne's proof of
158	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
159	morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) factoring a given pointed regular morphism
160	\(\varphi \colon C \to A\) through the canonical \(f_P \colon C \to J\) as follows. The
161	symmetric power \(C^{(g)}\) admits a regular morphism \(\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A\)
162	defined by
163	\[
164	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
165	\]
166	and a birational morphism \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J\) (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
167	seeks a morphism \(J \to A\) making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
168	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) followed by descent to \(J\); the rational map
169	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
170	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because \(C^{(g)}\) is
171	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and \(A\) is an abelian variety. Without
172	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
173	shortcut (\(J \cong J^\vee\)) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
174	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
175	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
176	
177	\section{Lean encoding}
178	\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}
179	
180	The Lean target file is the new
181	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}. The single declaration
182	this chapter targets is the theorem
183	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}, with signature (in informal
184	notation):
185	\[
186	  \texttt{X : Scheme} \;\to\; \texttt{[Smooth X over \(\bar k\)]} \;\to\;
187	  \texttt{A : AbelianVariety \(\bar k\)} \;\to\;
188	  (f : \texttt{X.RationalMap A}) \;\to\;
189	  \exists ! \, \widetilde f \colon X \to A, \;\; \widetilde f \;\text{extends}\; f.
190	\]
191	The proof body imports \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s exports and

194	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} respectively, and combines them

231	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the special cases ``rational map
232	    from a product of nonsingular varieties to an AV vanishing on the axes is constant'',

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	
95	\leanok
96	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
97	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
98	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
99	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
100	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
101	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
102	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
103	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
104	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
105	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
106	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
107	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
108	  % \varphi$."
109	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
110	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
111	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
112	  \[
113	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
114	  \]
115	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
116	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
117	  \[
118	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
119	  \]
120	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
121	
122	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
123	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
124	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
125	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
126	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
127	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
128	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
129	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
130	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
131	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
132	\end{definition}
133	
134	\begin{definition}
135	
136	
137	\leanok
138	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
139	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
141	  
142	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
143	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
144	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
145	  % PDF page 22).
146	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
147	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
148	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
149	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
150	  conclusion).}
151	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
152	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
153	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
154	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
155	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
156	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
157	
158	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
159	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
160	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
161	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
162	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
163	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
164	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
165	  is empty) as a single line.
166	
167	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
168	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
169	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
170	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
171	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
172	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
173	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
174	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
175	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
176	\end{definition}
177	
178	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
179	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
180	
181	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
182	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
183	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
184	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
185	
186	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
187	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
188	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
189	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
190	
191	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
192	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
193	
194	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
195	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
196	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a
197	stalk; smooth \(\Rightarrow\) admits a standard-smooth presentation
198	locally) are axiom-clean since iter-191; Stages~3--4 (the
199	scheme-to-algebra bridge for standard-smooth presentations; freeness of
200	\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\)) are
201	axiom-clean since iter-192. The remaining Stages~5--6 reduce the
202	Stacks~00TT conclusion to a freeness plus rank computation on K\"ahler
203	differentials, transported through localisation at the prime defining
204	the stalk. Iter-193 packaged Stages~5a (transport of freeness) and 5b
205	(matching rank under localisation) as the two standalone helpers below;
206	Stage~6 (the remaining Stacks~00OE smooth-algebra dimension formula
207	plus Stacks~02JK cotangent--K\"ahler-over-a-field bridge) is the open
208	Mathlib gap, decomposed iter-198 into the three named sub-lemmas
209	\cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (6.A),
210	\cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (6.B), and
211	\cref{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly} (6.C) in
212	\cref{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition} immediately following.
213	
214	\begin{lemma}
215	
216	
217	\leanok
218	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]
219	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
221	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
222	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
223	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
224	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
225	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
226	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
227	  \(S_M\)-module.
228	\end{lemma}
229	
230	\begin{proof}
231	  The canonical comparison map
232	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
233	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
234	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
235	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
236	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
237	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
238	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
239	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
240	  the conclusion.
241	\end{proof}
242	
243	\begin{lemma}
244	
245	
246	\leanok
247	  [Stage 5b --- K\"ahler-differentials rank under localisation equals
248	  the relative dimension]
249	  \label{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
250	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
251	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
252	  With notation as in \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},
253	  assume in addition that the \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is \emph{standard
254	  smooth of relative dimension \(n\)} (Mathlib's
255	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,\(n\)\,\(R\)\,\(S\),
256	  the algebra-side packaging of Stacks tag \texttt{00T7}(2)) and that
257	  the localisation \(S_M\) is non-trivial as a ring. Then the rank of
258	  \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) over \(S_M\) equals \(n\).
259	\end{lemma}
260	
261	\begin{proof}
262	  Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}
264	  asserts \(\mathrm{rank}_S(\Omega_{S/R}) = n\) under the
265	  standard-smooth hypothesis. The localisation map
266	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) is an
267	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} along \(S \to S_M\) (Stacks tag
268	  \texttt{02JK}; cf.\ the proof of
269	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}), and Mathlib's
270	  \texttt{Module.lift\_rank\_of\_isLocalizedModule\_of\_free} transports
271	  module rank through an \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} up to
272	  \(\mathrm{Cardinal.lift}\). Since \(n\) is a natural number both
273	  ranks equal \(n\), so
274	  \(\mathrm{rank}_{S_M}(\Omega_{S_M / R}) = n\) as required.
275	\end{proof}

277	\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
278	\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}
279	
280	Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
281	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
282	1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
283	\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
284	and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
285	\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
286	\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
287	\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
288	not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
289	Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
290	chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
291	and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
292	base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
293	Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
294	refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
295	ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
296	\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
297	q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
298	which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
299	Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
300	already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
301	named iter-199+ prover targets.
302	
303	\begin{lemma}
304	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
305	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
306	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
307	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
308	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
309	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
310	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
311	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
312	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
313	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
314	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
315	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
316	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
317	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
318	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
319	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
320	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
321	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
322	  \[
323	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
324	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
325	  \]
326	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
327	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
328	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
329	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
330	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
331	
332	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
333	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
334	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
335	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
336	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
337	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
338	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
339	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
340	\end{lemma}

362	  % $$I/I^2 \longrightarrow \Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \longrightarrow
363	  % \Omega_{S'/R} \longrightarrow 0$$
364	  % The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that $f \in I$ maps
365	  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
366	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
367	  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
368	  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
369	  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
370	  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
371	  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
372	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
373	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
374	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
375	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
376	  \[
377	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
378	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
379	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
380	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
381	  \]
382	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
383	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
384	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
385	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
386	  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
387	  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
388	  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
389	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
390	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
391	  \[
392	    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
393	  \]
394	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
395	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
396	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
397	\end{lemma}
398	
399	\begin{lemma}
400	  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
401	  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
402	  % NOTE (iter-199): the Lean target for this 6.C assembly is the body
403	  % of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing\_stalk\_of\_smooth}
404	  % itself (see \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}); the iter-198
405	  % blueprint named a planned \texttt{...\_aux} extraction that has not
406	  % been created in the Lean file. Treat the lemma below as the in-body
407	  % closure pattern that the existing helper consumes once Stages 6.A and
408	  % 6.B land, rather than as a separately-pinned Lean declaration.
409	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
410	        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
411	        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
412	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
413	  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
414	  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
415	  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
416	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
417	  regular local ring of Krull dimension
418	  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
419	  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
420	  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
421	  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
422	  dimension equals \(1\).
423	\end{lemma}
424	
425	\begin{proof}
426	  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
427	  \begin{enumerate}
428	    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
429	      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
430	      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
431	    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
432	      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
433	      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
434	      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
435	      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
436	      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
437	      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
438	      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
439	      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
440	    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
441	      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
442	      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
443	      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
444	      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
445	      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
446	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
447	      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
448	    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
449	      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
450	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
451	      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
452	      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
453	      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
454	      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
455	      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
456	      regular. The scheme-level transfer
457	      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
458	      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
459	      the conclusion.
460	  \end{enumerate}
461	\end{proof}
